title: Ensure that the --service-account-lookup argument is set to true

input_row.title: Ensure that the --service-account-lookup argument is set to true
input_row.description: Validate service account before validating token.
input_row.rationale: By default, the apiserver only verifies that the authentication token is valid. However, it does not validate that the service account token mentioned in the request is actually present in etcd. This allows using a service account token even after the corresponding service account is deleted. This is an example of time of check to time of use security issue.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --service-account-lookup argument exists and is set to true.
input_row.remediation: Edit the /etc/kubernetes/apiserver file on the master node and set the KUBE_API_ARGS parameter to "--service-account-lookup=true": KUBE_API_ARGS="--service-account-lookup=true" Based on your system, restart the kube-apiserver service. For example: systemctl restart kube-apiserver.service Impact: None Default Value: By default, --service-account-lookup argument is set to false.   59 | P a g e References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/24167 3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_of_check_to_time_of_use
input_row.impact: None Default Value: By default, --service-account-lookup argument is set to false.   59 | P a g e

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires verification of the kube-apiserver process startup arguments, specifically the presence and value of the --service-account-lookup flag. This flag is set via the process command line or configuration file on the host where the kube-apiserver runs. Kubernetes does not expose API server process flags or arguments through the Kubernetes API, kubectl, or any Kubernetes-native resource. Therefore, this check cannot be performed using kubectl or the Kubernetes API and requires direct access to the host OS or node running the kube-apiserver.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into the master node(s) where the kube-apiserver is running.
2. Run the following command to list the kube-apiserver process and its arguments:
   ```
   ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver
   ```
3. Review the output and verify that the --service-account-lookup argument is present and explicitly set to true (i.e., --service-account-lookup=true).
4. If the argument is missing or set to false, this is a finding. If it is present and set to true, the control is compliant.
5. If the kube-apiserver is managed by a systemd unit or a static Pod manifest (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml), you may also review the relevant configuration file to confirm the flag is set as required.
